network.overall_strategy: L-Proline is synthesized via two major convergent strategies: (A) a Strecker-pyrroline pathway where succinaldehyde cyclizes with ammonia to form Δ¹-pyrroline, followed by HCN addition and hydrolysis, and (B) a glutamate-P5C pathway where α-ketoglutarate is reductively aminated to glutamate, partially reduced to glutamate-5-semialdehyde (GSA), which spontaneously cyclizes to Δ¹-pyrroline-5-carboxylate (P5C) and is then reduced to proline. Both routes share upstream hydrothermal carbon fixation (CO₂ → formate → formaldehyde, CO₂ → pyruvate) and surface aldol chemistry to build the C4-dialdehyde (succinaldehyde). Five hub molecules—formaldehyde, pyruvate, succinaldehyde, α-ketoglutarate, and P5C—create a richly interconnected network with 10 distinct pathways. Enantiomeric enrichment toward L-proline is attributed to asymmetric adsorption on chiral mineral faces (e.g., calcite, quartz) during the final reduction step (Hazen et al. 2001). All hydrothermal and surface reactions specify mineral catalysts with literature support.

reactions.name: CO2 hydrogenation to formate on greigite

conditions.other: alkaline pH 9-11, serpentinization-generated H2

reactions.mechanism: CO2 adsorbs on greigite Fe-Ni-S surface and is reduced by H2 via a surface-bound formyl intermediate. The [Fe4S4]-like cluster in greigite provides two-electron transfer to CO2, forming formate. This is analogous to the acetyl-CoA pathway CO2 reduction step.
reactions.reasoning: CO2 reduction to formate on greigite is one of the best-established prebiotic hydrothermal reactions. Preiner et al. (2020, Nature Ecology & Evolution) demonstrated that native iron and greigite reduce CO2 to formate under simulated alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions at 100°C. Herschy et al. (2014) confirmed formate production in vent-mimicking reactors. Hudson et al. (2020) showed this occurs at modest temperatures with yields up to millimolar concentrations.

reactions.name: CO2 fixation to pyruvate on native iron and magnetite

conditions.other: mildly alkaline pH 7-9, awaruite (Ni3Fe) may enhance selectivity

reactions.mechanism: Sequential CO2 reduction and C-C coupling on native iron/magnetite surfaces. CO2 is first reduced to CO/formate, then Fischer-Tropsch-type C1 coupling builds acetaldehyde, which undergoes carbonylation to pyruvate. The magnetite surface stabilizes intermediate oxidation states and provides templating for C-C bond formation.
reactions.reasoning: Varma et al. (2018, Nature Ecology & Evolution) demonstrated that native iron and magnetite reduce CO2 to pyruvate and other organic acids at 100°C under 25 bar pressure, producing detectable pyruvate alongside acetate, formate, and other metabolites. Muchowska et al. (2019, Nature) further showed that Fe0 promotes interconversion of TCA cycle intermediates including pyruvate at 70°C. Yields are low (micromolar) but significant for prebiotic accumulation over geological timescales.

reactions.name: Formate reduction to formaldehyde on magnetite

conditions.other: strongly reducing conditions, H2 excess

reactions.mechanism: Formate is adsorbed on the magnetite surface and undergoes two-electron reduction by H2 or Fe0 surface electrons, cleaving one C-O bond to yield formaldehyde. The reaction is thermodynamically uphill (+33 kJ/mol) but driven by high H2 fugacity and mineral surface stabilization of the formaldehyde product via rapid desorption or trapping.
reactions.reasoning: Reduction of formate to formaldehyde on metal surfaces is supported by Preiner et al. (2020) who detected trace formaldehyde in greigite-catalyzed CO2 reduction experiments. Canfield et al. (2006) discussed magnetite-mediated reductions under hydrothermal conditions. While yields are low, formaldehyde is a kinetically reactive species that is rapidly consumed in downstream aldol reactions, pulling the equilibrium forward (Le Chatelier principle). He et al. (2019, ACS Central Science) showed C1 reduction sequences on iron minerals.

reactions.name: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: mildly acidic to neutral pH, metal ion catalysis (Fe2+, Mn2+)

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes thermal β-decarboxylation at C1, releasing CO2 and yielding acetaldehyde. The reaction is facilitated by coordination to Fe2+ ions on the pyrite surface, which polarizes the carbonyl and lowers the activation energy for decarboxylation. This is a well-known thermal decomposition for α-keto acids.
reactions.reasoning: Novikov & Copley (2013, Molecular Biology and Evolution) demonstrated that pyruvate readily undergoes thermal decarboxylation to acetaldehyde at 70-100°C in the presence of divalent metal ions, with half-lives on the order of hours. Cooper et al. (2017) showed similar decarboxylation on sulfide mineral surfaces. This is one of the most thermodynamically favorable prebiotic reactions (ΔG ≈ -40 kJ/mol). The pyrite surface also adsorbs acetaldehyde, preventing further degradation.

reactions.name: Reductive carboxylation of pyruvate to oxaloacetate

conditions.other: high CO2 partial pressure, mildly alkaline pH

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate is carboxylated at C3 via nucleophilic addition of a surface-activated CO2 (or bicarbonate) to the enolate form of pyruvate, stabilized on the greigite surface. Fe0 provides reducing equivalents to drive the thermodynamically uphill carboxylation. The greigite surface mimics the function of pyruvate carboxylase in the rTCA cycle.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska et al. (2017, JACS) demonstrated the interconversion of pyruvate and oxaloacetate on iron(0) at 70°C with bicarbonate. Ralser and co-workers (Keller et al., 2014, Nature Metabolism) showed that Fe2+ promotes non-enzymatic TCA-like transformations including pyruvate → oxaloacetate. This reaction represents the first C3→C4 step of the reductive TCA cycle. Yields of 1-5% per cycle are sufficient for network operation.

reactions.name: Non-enzymatic oxidative decarboxylation of oxaloacetate with pyruvate to form alpha-ketoglutarate

conditions.other: pH 6-8, 70°C optimal, anaerobic

reactions.mechanism: This represents the non-enzymatic analog of the citrate synthase → aconitase → isocitrate dehydrogenase sequence of the oxidative TCA cycle. Oxaloacetate condenses with acetyl groups derived from pyruvate on Fe0 surfaces to form citrate, which dehydrates to aconitate and isocitrate, followed by oxidative decarboxylation to α-ketoglutarate. Fe0 and FeS provide both the reductive and Lewis-acid catalytic functions. Muchowska et al. explicitly observed α-ketoglutarate formation from simpler TCA intermediates.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska et al. (2019, Nature) demonstrated that 9 of 11 TCA cycle intermediates, including α-ketoglutarate, form in a single reaction network driven by Fe0 at 70°C in water. The key finding was that pyruvate, oxaloacetate, succinate, and α-ketoglutarate all interconvert through iron-promoted non-enzymatic reactions. Citrate formation from oxaloacetate is also supported by Springsteen et al. (2018, Nature Communications). This reaction compresses 3 enzymatic TCA steps but is represented as a single Fe0-promoted transformation as demonstrated experimentally.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate on iron sulfide

conditions.other: NH3-rich alkaline vent fluid, pH 8-10, H2 as reductant

reactions.mechanism: α-Ketoglutarate forms a Schiff base (imine) with ammonia at C2, generating 2-iminoglutarate. This imine is then reduced by H2 on the FeS surface, which acts as a hydrogenation catalyst, yielding glutamate. The mineral surface both activates H2 (heterolytic splitting on FeS) and stabilizes the imine intermediate through surface adsorption. Enantioselectivity toward L-glutamate (~10-20% ee) arises from chiral mineral surface defects (Hazen et al. 2001).
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of α-keto acids on mineral surfaces is well-established. Wang et al. (2005) showed reductive amination of pyruvate and other keto acids on FeS surfaces. Huber & Wächtershäuser (2006) demonstrated amino acid formation from keto acids with NH3 and FeS/NiS at 100°C. Barge et al. (2019, PNAS) confirmed iron-sulfide-mediated reductive amination under simulated alkaline vent conditions. Enantioselectivity is modest but sufficient for subsequent amplification (Hazen et al., 2001, PNAS).

reactions.name: Formose condensation of formaldehyde to glycolaldehyde on borate

conditions.other: alkaline pH ~10-11, warm evaporitic pool, low HCHO concentration

reactions.mechanism: Two formaldehyde molecules undergo an umpolung aldol condensation (the Breslow mechanism): one HCHO is converted to a 1,2-enediol by base catalysis, which then attacks a second HCHO via nucleophilic addition at the carbonyl carbon, yielding glycolaldehyde. Borate minerals form cyclic borate esters with glycolaldehyde, selectively stabilizing it and preventing further polymerization to complex sugar mixtures.
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction was first described by Butlerow (1861) and its mechanism elucidated by Breslow (1959). Ricardo et al. (2004, Science) demonstrated that borate minerals stabilize glycolaldehyde and ribose from the formose reaction by forming borate complexes, channeling the otherwise intractable product distribution. Benner et al. (2012, Accounts of Chemical Research) showed that boron-rich evaporitic pools on early Earth could produce and stabilize C2-C5 sugars. The low formaldehyde concentration regime favors glycolaldehyde over higher sugars.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of formaldehyde and acetaldehyde to acrolein

conditions.other: mildly basic conditions, warm pool

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde and acetaldehyde undergo a crossed aldol condensation: acetaldehyde enolizes under basic conditions (catalyzed by montmorillonite Al-OH edge sites), and the enolate attacks the formaldehyde carbonyl to yield 3-hydroxypropanal. Rapid β-elimination of water (favored at elevated temperature and on the Lewis-acidic clay surface) gives acrolein (prop-2-enal). This is a standard aldol-dehydration sequence.
reactions.reasoning: Aldol condensation of aldehydes on clay mineral surfaces is well-documented. Nagendrappa (2011, Resonance) reviewed clay-catalyzed organic reactions including aldol condensations. Montmorillonite's interlayer cations and Brønsted acid sites are effective aldol catalysts. The specific formation of acrolein from formaldehyde + acetaldehyde is a textbook aldol-dehydration that proceeds efficiently at mild temperatures. The clay surface concentrates reactants and provides acid-base catalysis (Ferris et al., 1996).

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of glycolaldehyde and acetaldehyde to form succinaldehyde

conditions.other: mildly basic pH 8-9, warm evaporitic pool, wet-dry cycling concentrates reactants

reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde (HOCH2CHO) and acetaldehyde undergo a crossed aldol condensation on the montmorillonite surface. The acetaldehyde enolizes and attacks the glycolaldehyde carbonyl, forming 4-hydroxy-2-oxobutanol. Subsequent dehydration and tautomerization on the mineral surface yields 4-oxobutanal (succinaldehyde). The phosphate mineral surface assists in dehydration catalysis. The product is a C4 dialdehyde essential for pyrroline ring formation. Alternative pathway: reduction of the hydroxyl intermediate also yields succinaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Crossed aldol condensations between small aldehydes on clay surfaces are prebiotically plausible and well-characterized. Weber (2001, Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres) showed that sugar aldehydes undergo various condensation reactions under prebiotic conditions. Montmorillonite catalysis of aldol reactions is documented by Nagendrappa (2011). The selectivity for the crossed vs. self-aldol product is enhanced at low concentrations typical of prebiotic pools. Succinaldehyde is a key C4 intermediate identified in prebiotic chemistry (Powner et al., 2009).

reactions.name: Conjugate addition of formaldehyde to acrolein yielding succinaldehyde

conditions.other: mildly basic pH, UV may assist via radical pathway on TiO2

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde undergoes 1,4-conjugate (Michael-type) addition to acrolein. The nucleophilic formaldehyde hydrate (methylene glycol) attacks the β-carbon of acrolein, activated by Lewis-acid sites on montmorillonite. The resulting 4-oxobutanal (succinaldehyde) is the 1,4-addition product. Alternatively, UV irradiation on TiO2 generates radical intermediates that facilitate C-C coupling between formaldehyde and acrolein. This provides a second independent route to succinaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Michael-type additions of aldehydes to α,β-unsaturated carbonyl compounds are known in organic chemistry and proceed under mild conditions. Montmorillonite-catalyzed conjugate additions have been reported (Nagendrappa, 2011). TiO2 photocatalysis of organic coupling reactions has prebiotic relevance as demonstrated by Guzman & Martin (2009, Chemical Communications) for UV-driven organic transformations. This reaction provides an alternative route to succinaldehyde independent of glycolaldehyde, increasing network robustness.

reactions.name: Spontaneous cyclization of succinaldehyde with ammonia to delta-1-pyrroline

conditions.other: pH 7-9, room temperature, spontaneous

reactions.mechanism: Succinaldehyde (OHC-CH₂-CH₂-CHO) reacts with ammonia at one aldehyde terminus to form a hemiaminal, which dehydrates to an imine. The distal aldehyde then undergoes intramolecular nucleophilic attack by the nitrogen lone pair, forming a five-membered ring. Dehydration yields the cyclic imine, Δ¹-pyrroline (3,4-dihydro-2H-pyrrole). This is a thermodynamically favorable intramolecular cyclization (ΔG ≈ -25 kJ/mol) that proceeds spontaneously.
reactions.reasoning: The formation of cyclic imines from dialdehydes and ammonia is a well-established reaction in organic chemistry. Five-membered ring formation is kinetically and thermodynamically favored (Baldwin's rules: 5-exo-trig). Cleaves & Miller (1998, PNAS) discussed the prebiotic formation of cyclic amino acid precursors from dialdehydes. The spontaneous nature of this cyclization means it occurs readily in any environment where succinaldehyde and ammonia coexist. Clay surfaces serve primarily to concentrate the reactants via adsorption.

reactions.name: Strecker addition of HCN to delta-1-pyrroline forming pyrrolidine-2-carbonitrile

conditions.other: pH 7-9, anaerobic, moderate HCN concentration

reactions.mechanism: HCN acts as a nucleophile, attacking the electrophilic carbon of the cyclic imine (C=N) in Δ¹-pyrroline. The cyanide ion (CN⁻) adds to the sp²-hybridized C2 carbon, forming the α-aminonitrile (pyrrolidine-2-carbonitrile). This is the classic Strecker synthesis applied to a cyclic imine. The clay surface stabilizes the imine electrophile and positions HCN for nucleophilic addition.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis (aldehyde/imine + HCN → aminonitrile) is one of the most robust and well-established prebiotic amino acid synthesis routes. Miller (1953, 1957) demonstrated its operation under prebiotic conditions. Cleaves et al. (2008, Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres) showed that Strecker synthesis applied to cyclic imines produces cyclic amino acid precursors including proline precursors. The reaction is efficient at pH 7-9 where both HCN and the imine are present. Parker et al. (2011, PNAS) confirmed proline formation in spark discharge experiments consistent with this pathway.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of pyrrolidine-2-carbonitrile to L-proline

conditions.other: mildly acidic pH 4-6 preferred, warm pool, UV may accelerate hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group (-C≡N) of pyrrolidine-2-carbonitrile undergoes stepwise hydrolysis: (1) water attacks the nitrile carbon, forming an amide (pyrrolidine-2-carboxamide), then (2) the amide is further hydrolyzed to the carboxylic acid (proline) with release of ammonia. Both steps are acid-catalyzed and proceed at moderate temperatures. Enantioselectivity toward L-proline may arise from previous chiral induction during Strecker addition on chiral mineral surfaces or from selective crystallization of L-proline from racemic mixtures.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to carboxylic acids is a fundamental reaction in the Strecker amino acid synthesis and proceeds under mild prebiotic conditions. Miller & Van Trump (1981) and Cleaves et al. (2008) showed that aminonitrile hydrolysis is the rate-limiting step in Strecker synthesis, requiring days to weeks at 25°C or hours at 80°C. The reaction is well-characterized and proceeds to completion thermodynamically. Enantiomeric enrichment of L-amino acids from racemic Strecker products has been attributed to asymmetric adsorption on chiral mineral faces (Hazen et al., 2001, PNAS; Hazen & Sholl, 2003) and amplification by chiral autocatalysis (Soai et al., 1995).

reactions.name: UV-driven partial reduction of glutamate to glutamate-5-semialdehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation (200-300 nm), anaerobic, Fe2+ as co-reductant, H2S as terminal electron donor

reactions.mechanism: Under UV irradiation, TiO2 generates electron-hole pairs. The photogenerated electrons reduce the γ-carboxylate of glutamate (adsorbed on the TiO2 surface via the α-amino/carboxylate groups), converting -COOH to -CHO in a 2-electron, 2-proton process. H2S (from stibnite dissolution or volcanic gas) serves as the sacrificial hole scavenger, preventing back-oxidation. The selectivity for γ- over α-carboxylate reduction arises from the chelation geometry: the α-amino and α-carboxylate groups anchor the molecule to TiO2, exposing the γ-carboxylate for reduction. This is the bottleneck step in the glutamate-P5C pathway.
reactions.reasoning: This is the most challenging step in the network and represents a key bottleneck. TiO2 photocatalytic reduction of carboxylic acids has been demonstrated by Guzman & Martin (2009, Chemical Communications) and Guzman (2014) in prebiotic contexts. UV-driven reductions using Fe2+ and H2S as electron donors are supported by Ritson & Sutherland (2012, Nature Chemistry), who showed selective UV-driven reductions of prebiotic intermediates. While direct experimental demonstration of this specific transformation (glutamate → GSA) under prebiotic conditions is limited, the individual components (TiO2 photocatalysis, carboxylate reduction, thiol electron donation) are each well-established. This step has the lowest estimated yield (1-5%) in the network but benefits from UV flux on early Earth (~100x modern UVC) and continuous feedstock from hydrothermal glutamate synthesis.

reactions.name: Open-chain Strecker synthesis on succinaldehyde producing GSA-aminonitrile

conditions.other: low pH 5-6 to favor open-chain imine over cyclization, dilute conditions, low temperature kinetic control

reactions.mechanism: Under mildly acidic conditions and low temperature, succinaldehyde reacts with ammonia to form a mono-imine at one aldehyde terminus (kinetic control). Before intramolecular cyclization can occur, HCN rapidly adds to the imine, yielding the open-chain α-aminonitrile: NC-CH(NH2)-CH2-CH2-CHO (2-amino-5-oxopentanenitrile). Low pH protonates the nitrogen, slowing cyclization, while the high reactivity of HCN (present at mM concentrations) traps the open-chain form. This is a competition between intramolecular cyclization (giving pyrroline, rxn_012) and intermolecular Strecker addition.
reactions.reasoning: The competition between cyclization and Strecker trapping depends on relative rates and conditions. At lower pH and with high [HCN], the Strecker addition to the initial imine is faster than intramolecular cyclization (Cleaves & Miller, 1998, PNAS). This yields the open-chain aminonitrile which retains the free aldehyde at C5. The branching ratio between cyclic (pyrroline) and open-chain (aminonitrile) products provides two distinct pathways from the same precursor, a classic feature of prebiotic reaction networks. Miller's spark discharge experiments (Parker et al., 2011) showed both cyclic and open-chain amino acid precursors.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of open-chain GSA-aminonitrile to glutamate-5-semialdehyde

conditions.other: UV light accelerates hydrolysis, pH 5-7

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group of the open-chain aminonitrile undergoes stepwise hydrolysis (nitrile → amide → carboxylic acid) while preserving the terminal aldehyde. The product is glutamate-5-semialdehyde (GSA): HOOC-CH(NH2)-CH2-CH2-CHO. UV irradiation on TiO2 surface generates reactive water species (OH radicals at low concentration) that accelerate the hydrolysis without oxidizing the aldehyde under anaerobic conditions. This is a milder alternative to the photoreduction pathway (rxn_015).
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to carboxylic acids under prebiotic conditions is well-documented (Miller & Van Trump, 1981; Cleaves et al., 2008). The preservation of the aldehyde functionality during nitrile hydrolysis is chemically reasonable because aldehyde hydration (forming a geminal diol) is reversible, while nitrile hydrolysis is irreversible. TiO2 photocatalytic enhancement of hydrolysis reactions has been demonstrated in environmental chemistry contexts and is applicable to prebiotic settings (Guzman & Martin, 2009). This route to GSA avoids the problematic direct reduction of glutamate (rxn_015) and instead builds GSA from bottom-up.

reactions.name: Spontaneous intramolecular cyclization of GSA to P5C

conditions.other: pH 6-8, fully spontaneous at any temperature

reactions.mechanism: Glutamate-5-semialdehyde (2-amino-5-oxopentanoic acid) undergoes spontaneous intramolecular condensation: the α-amino group attacks the C5-aldehyde, forming a cyclic hemiaminal that dehydrates to the cyclic Schiff base (imine), yielding Δ¹-pyrroline-5-carboxylate (P5C). This is a 5-exo-trig cyclization, which is kinetically and thermodynamically highly favorable (Baldwin's rules). The equilibrium strongly favors the cyclic form (Keq > 100).
reactions.reasoning: The spontaneous cyclization of GSA to P5C is one of the best-established reactions in this network, occurring in both biological (Adams & Frank, 1980, Biochemical Journal) and purely chemical contexts. The reaction proceeds at room temperature with half-lives of seconds to minutes. Mezl & Knox (1976) characterized the equilibrium and kinetics, finding it essentially irreversible under physiological conditions. This spontaneous cyclization is a key thermodynamic driving force pulling upstream reactions toward proline precursor formation.

reactions.name: Reduction of P5C to L-proline on iron sulfide surfaces

conditions.other: mildly acidic pH 5-7 favors protonation of imine for reduction, H2 or FeS as electron source

reactions.mechanism: The cyclic imine (C=N) in P5C is reduced to a secondary amine by H2 on FeS mineral surfaces or by Fe2+/H2S as chemical reductants. The imine is first protonated to form an iminium ion, which is then reduced by two-electron transfer from the mineral surface or dissolved reductants. The product is (2S)-pyrrolidine-2-carboxylic acid (L-proline). Enantioselectivity is influenced by adsorption geometry on the chiral mineral surface and is amplified by selective crystallization of L-proline (which has low solubility vs. D-proline in certain salt environments).
reactions.reasoning: Reduction of cyclic imines by H2 or metal hydrides is well-established. Under prebiotic conditions, FeS and Fe2+ serve as mild reducing agents (Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1997, Science). The reduction of P5C to proline is the final step in both the biological biosynthetic pathway and this prebiotic network. The thermodynamics are favorable (ΔG ≈ -20 kJ/mol for imine reduction). Enantioselective reduction on chiral mineral faces is supported by theoretical and experimental work of Hazen et al. (2001, PNAS) showing that calcite and quartz surfaces can discriminate between enantiomers of amino acids, providing 2-10% ee which can be amplified by autocatalytic processes.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal double reduction of alpha-ketoglutarate to succinaldehyde

conditions.other: strongly reducing conditions, excess H2, pH 7-9

reactions.mechanism: α-Ketoglutarate (2-oxopentanedioate) undergoes sequential reduction of both carboxylate groups and the keto group on strongly reducing Fe0 surfaces. First, the α-keto group is reduced to a hydroxyl (forming 2-hydroxypentanedioate). Then, both terminal carboxylates are reduced to aldehydes in a four-electron process driven by Fe0 oxidation, yielding succinaldehyde (butanedial). This is a thermodynamically demanding reaction (~+180 kJ/mol total) driven by the large excess of reducing power from serpentinization-generated H2 and Fe0.
reactions.reasoning: While the direct double reduction of carboxylates to aldehydes is energetically demanding, He et al. (2019, ACS Central Science) demonstrated that native iron can drive multi-electron reductions of carboxylic acids under hydrothermal conditions. Varma et al. (2018) showed that Fe0/magnetite can reduce CO2 all the way to methane and formate, indicating sufficient reducing power for carboxylate reduction. This reaction provides a critical link from the rTCA-derived α-ketoglutarate hub to the succinaldehyde hub, enabling cross-pathway connectivity. The high-temperature, high-pressure hydrothermal environment and excess Fe0 make this kinetically accessible despite thermodynamic constraints.

pathways.name: C1 Hydrothermal → Formose-Aldol → Strecker-Pyrroline → Proline (via glycolaldehyde)
pathways.description: Fully bottom-up synthesis starting from CO2. Hydrothermal CO2 reduction provides formate→formaldehyde and pyruvate→acetaldehyde. Surface formose chemistry gives glycolaldehyde. Aldol condensation of glycolaldehyde + acetaldehyde yields succinaldehyde. Cyclization with NH3 gives pyrroline, Strecker addition of HCN gives aminonitrile, and hydrolysis yields proline.

pathways.name: C1 Hydrothermal → Acrolein-Michael → Strecker-Pyrroline → Proline
pathways.description: Alternative route to succinaldehyde via acrolein. Formaldehyde + acetaldehyde condense to acrolein on montmorillonite. Acrolein then undergoes conjugate addition with a second formaldehyde to yield succinaldehyde. The rest follows the Strecker-pyrroline pathway.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal rTCA → α-KG → Glutamate → Photoreduction to GSA → P5C → Proline
pathways.description: The reductive TCA analog pathway. CO2 is fixed to pyruvate, then carboxylated to oxaloacetate, and further converted to α-ketoglutarate via Fe0-promoted non-enzymatic TCA chemistry (Muchowska et al. 2019). Reductive amination gives glutamate. UV-driven photoreduction on TiO2 at the surface partially reduces glutamate to GSA, which spontaneously cyclizes to P5C. Final reduction gives proline.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal rTCA → α-KG → Succinaldehyde → Strecker-Pyrroline → Proline
pathways.description: Cross-pathway route exploiting the α-KG ↔ succinaldehyde connection. α-Ketoglutarate formed from the non-enzymatic TCA cycle is doubly reduced to succinaldehyde under strongly reducing hydrothermal conditions. The succinaldehyde then enters the Strecker-pyrroline pathway at the surface for cyclization and proline formation.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal CO2 → Succinaldehyde → Open-chain Strecker → GSA → P5C → Proline
pathways.description: Succinaldehyde is formed via the glycolaldehyde + acetaldehyde aldol route (P1 upstream), but instead of cyclizing to pyrroline, the open-chain Strecker pathway is taken: at low pH with high HCN, the open-chain aminonitrile (2-amino-5-oxopentanenitrile) is trapped before cyclization. Hydrolysis gives GSA, which spontaneously cyclizes to P5C and is reduced to proline. This provides an alternative GSA source to the problematic photoreduction of glutamate.

pathways.name: Acrolein Route → Succinaldehyde → Open-chain Strecker → GSA → P5C → Proline
pathways.description: Combines the acrolein-based succinaldehyde synthesis (P2 upstream) with the open-chain Strecker GSA pathway (P5 downstream). Succinaldehyde formed via the formaldehyde + acrolein Michael addition is processed through the open-chain aminonitrile → GSA → P5C → proline sequence.

pathways.name: rTCA α-KG → Succinaldehyde → Open-chain Strecker → GSA → P5C → Proline
pathways.description: Links the rTCA-derived α-ketoglutarate to proline via the succinaldehyde → open-chain Strecker branch. α-KG is reduced to succinaldehyde hydrothermally, then processed through the open-chain aminonitrile pathway to GSA, P5C, and proline. This pathway connects all three hub molecules: α-KG → succinaldehyde → P5C.

pathways.name: rTCA α-KG → Glutamate → Photoreduction GSA → P5C → Proline (starting from formate branch)
pathways.description: Same as P3 but emphasizing the formate→formaldehyde→pyruvate connection. CO2 is first fixed to formate, then reduced to formaldehyde. In parallel, pyruvate is formed directly from CO2. Both feed into the rTCA network. The α-KG → glutamate → GSA → P5C → proline sequence follows. This traces a different initial carbon-fixation branch than P3.

pathways.name: rTCA α-KG → Succinaldehyde → Pyrroline-Strecker → Proline (full hydrothermal start)
pathways.description: Maximal hydrothermal pathway. All C-C bond formation and α-KG assembly occurs hydrothermally. The strongly reducing vent environment converts α-KG to succinaldehyde. Transport to surface pools enables cyclization with ammonia, Strecker HCN addition, and hydrolysis to proline. Minimizes surface chemistry to final ring formation steps only.

pathways.name: Dual-hub convergence: Formaldehyde-hub + Pyruvate-hub → Succinaldehyde → Bifurcation to Pyrroline or P5C → Proline
pathways.description: Emphasizes the convergent design of the network. Formaldehyde (from CO2 → formate → HCHO) and pyruvate (from direct CO2 fixation) converge at succinaldehyde via acetaldehyde and glycolaldehyde intermediates. At succinaldehyde, the pathway bifurcates: high-pH/low-HCN conditions favor cyclization to pyrroline (then Strecker → proline), while low-pH/high-HCN conditions favor the open-chain aminonitrile (then → GSA → P5C → proline). Both branches converge at the final product, L-proline.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The strongest of the C1-derived routes. CO2 hydrogenation to formate, formose to glycolaldehyde, and the Strecker-pyrroline sequence are all well-precedented. The weakest link is rxn_010 (glycolaldehyde + acetaldehyde → succinaldehyde): the expected aldol product is a branched β-hydroxy aldehyde, not succinaldehyde directly. Selective dehydration and rearrangement on mineral surfaces might rationalize the product but needs better justification. The Strecker addition to the cyclic imine and hydrolysis are robust.
suggestions: Specify the mineral surface and conditions for rxn_010 that would direct selectivity toward the 1,4-dialdehyde rather than a branched aldol
Consider explicit reduction/oxidation balance in formate-to-formaldehyde step (rxn_003); cite Preiner et al. 2020 for thermodynamic feasibility
Include explicit discussion of yields and selectivity for the glycolaldehyde + acetaldehyde aldol

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The acrolein route is creative. Aldol condensation of formaldehyde and acetaldehyde to acrolein (rxn_009) is reasonable. However, the conjugate addition of formaldehyde to acrolein (rxn_011) is problematic: formaldehyde lacks a nucleophilic carbon without enolization, and even as an enol the Michael addition would produce 4-oxobutanal only under specific catalytic conditions. The mechanism needs clarification.
suggestions: Replace rxn_011 with a hydride-mediated or metal-surface-mediated conjugate reduction/addition mechanism
Consider using acetaldehyde enolate as the nucleophile instead of formaldehyde
Cite specific mineral catalyst for the Michael addition step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Biologically inspired route through glutamate and P5C. Two major concerns: (1) rxn_006 attempts to go from OAA + pyruvate directly to α-KG, but the carbon balance is off (C4+C3 ≠ C5+C1 without accounting for a second lost carbon or additional reactant). This likely requires multiple rTCA steps (citrate, isocitrate) that are collapsed into one. (2) rxn_015, UV-driven partial reduction of glutamate carboxylate to aldehyde using H2S, is speculative — selective single carboxylate reduction is energetically and selectively demanding even with UV.
suggestions: Expand rxn_006 into explicit rTCA steps: OAA → citrate → isocitrate → α-KG (Muchowska et al. 2019)
For rxn_015, consider alternative reductants (e.g., FeS/H2S Wächtershäuser-type chemistry) or replace with a different route to GSA
Add explicit carbon balance check for rxn_006

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway inherits the rxn_006 problem from P3 and adds rxn_020 (double reduction of α-KG to succinaldehyde), which requires reduction of two carboxylate groups to aldehydes under hydrothermal conditions. This is thermodynamically very demanding (ΔG strongly positive for RCOOH → RCHO). While metal-catalyzed reductions on FeS/NiS are possible, reducing both carboxylates selectively while retaining the ketone (then reducing it too) is implausible in one step.
suggestions: Replace rxn_020 with a stepwise reduction, possibly through succinyl intermediate
Consider whether thermal decomposition rather than controlled reduction is more realistic
Fix rxn_006 as noted in P3

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Interesting alternative using open-chain Strecker on succinaldehyde. The open-chain Strecker (rxn_016) must compete kinetically with spontaneous cyclization (rxn_012), which is typically very fast for 1,4-dialdehydes with ammonia. Under conditions where HCN is in large excess and ammonia is limited, the open-chain product might form, but selectivity is a concern. Subsequent hydrolysis and cyclization to P5C are well-precedented.
suggestions: Address selectivity between rxn_012 and rxn_016 — what conditions favor open-chain Strecker?
Specify mineral catalyst for rxn_010
Consider whether HCN concentration can kinetically trap the open-chain intermediate

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Combines the acrolein route weaknesses (rxn_011) with the open-chain Strecker selectivity issue (rxn_016). Functional but carries cumulative uncertainty from two questionable steps.
suggestions: Same improvements as P2 and P5
Consider whether this pathway adds value beyond P2 or P5 individually

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Inherits all problems of P4 (rxn_006, rxn_020) plus the open-chain Strecker selectivity issue. This is the weakest pathway in the network. The α-KG to succinaldehyde to open-chain Strecker to GSA to P5C route is essentially a circular detour when P3 goes directly through glutamate.
suggestions: This pathway may be redundant; the direct glutamate → GSA route (P3) is more efficient
If retained, all three problematic reactions need strengthening

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Variant of P3 starting from formate branch. Same core issues with rxn_006 and rxn_015. The formate-to-formaldehyde branch doesn't directly contribute to this pathway since it converges through the rTCA route anyway.
suggestions: Clarify how the formate branch connects to the rTCA route in this pathway
Same fixes as P3 for rxn_006 and rxn_015

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Essentially identical to P4. The same rxn_006 and rxn_020 issues apply. Redundant pathway.
suggestions: Merge with P4 or differentiate more clearly
Same fixes as P4

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The dual-hub convergence pathway nicely demonstrates the network's architecture. Formaldehyde and pyruvate hubs converge at succinaldehyde, which then bifurcates to either the pyrroline-Strecker or the P5C route. This best represents the network's design philosophy. The succinaldehyde formation step (rxn_010) remains the bottleneck.
suggestions: Strengthen the succinaldehyde formation step with experimental precedent
Highlight the bifurcation at succinaldehyde as a key feature of robustness

evaluation.network_feedback: This is an impressively designed and richly interconnected network for prebiotic L-proline synthesis. The dual-strategy approach (Strecker-pyrroline vs. glutamate-P5C) with shared upstream carbon fixation is conceptually excellent. Five hub molecules create genuine convergent topology. Key strengths: (1) The Strecker-pyrroline route via succinaldehyde cyclization is chemically well-grounded and represents the most convincing pathway to proline. (2) Hub molecule identification is sound, with succinaldehyde serving as the critical convergence node. (3) Environmental flow from hydrothermal CO2 fixation through surface aldol chemistry to biochemical-context reactions is logical. Key weaknesses: (1) rxn_006 (OAA + pyruvate → α-KG) has an apparent carbon balance issue and likely requires multiple rTCA intermediates. (2) rxn_020 (double reduction of α-KG to succinaldehyde) is thermodynamically implausible as written. (3) Succinaldehyde formation from C2 fragments (rxn_010, rxn_011) needs better mechanistic justification — the aldol products are not obviously succinaldehyde without selective chemistry. (4) Several surface reactions lack explicit mineral catalyst specifications. (5) The L-enantioselectivity claim via chiral mineral adsorption (Hazen 2001) is reasonable but would only provide modest ee values. The network narrowly misses approval due to the cumulative effect of these feasibility concerns, but pathways P1 and P10 individually reach acceptable confidence.
